concepts.title: Outbound Caller ID in Zoom Phone
concepts.summary: A configurable setting that determines which phone number appears on a recipient's caller ID display when a Zoom Phone user makes an outbound call. Users can select from numbers assigned to their account, the company main number, or call queues they belong to. The UX must clearly present available options within the 100-number dropdown limit and explain why certain numbers may not appear.
key_points: Caller ID can be set to user's own number, company main number, or call queue numbers
Selection is restricted to numbers the user is explicitly associated with
Dropdown has a maximum display limit of 100 phone numbers
Auto-receptionist numbers can be used as outbound caller ID with proper configuration

concepts.title: E.164 Caller ID Formatting
concepts.summary: Zoom Phone uses E.164 international formatting for all caller ID display, which includes country code prefixes by default and cannot be disabled. Designers must account for the fact that display behavior also depends on the recipient's device, carrier, and phone settings, meaning the caller ID format is not fully controllable.
key_points: Country code prefixes are always included and cannot be removed
Japanese +81 prefix is specifically called out as immutable
Display varies by recipient device, carrier, and phone settings
No per-user option to suppress international formatting

concepts.title: Call Queue Membership and Caller ID
concepts.summary: A user's ability to select a call queue number as their outbound caller ID depends directly on whether they are a member of that call queue. If a user needs to display a call queue number, an admin must first add them as a member. This membership-based availability model creates a dependency that the UI should make transparent.
key_points: Only call-queue member numbers appear in the outbound caller ID dropdown
Admins must add users to a call queue before the number becomes selectable
Users who leave a call queue lose access to that number as caller ID
Multiple call queue numbers can be added as caller ID options per user

concepts.title: Auto-Receptionist Caller ID Configuration
concepts.summary: Auto-receptionist numbers can serve as outbound caller IDs, but improper configuration may cause intermittent call failures. The caller ID must be explicitly set in the user's profile, and the feature requires correct policy and calling plan alignment. Designers should consider warning indicators when an auto-receptionist number may cause issues.
key_points: Auto-receptionist numbers can be used as outbound caller ID
Incorrect configuration can lead to intermittent call failures
Requires explicit setup in user profile under Outbound Caller ID settings
Post-configuration testing is recommended to verify calls succeed

user_roles.title: Account Owner / Admin
user_roles.summary: Admins manage caller ID configuration across the organization including auto-receptionist caller ID setup, call queue member management, and resolving caller ID visibility issues. Their workflow often involves diagnosing why a user cannot see a number (usually membership or policy restrictions) and making structural changes rather than per-call adjustments.
key_points: Configures auto-receptionist caller ID settings for users
Adds and removes users from call queues to enable caller ID options
Manages outbound caller ID at the profile level for individual phone users
Troubleshoots call failures related to caller ID misconfiguration

user_roles.title: Phone User (Call Queue Member)
user_roles.summary: Phone users select their outbound caller ID from available numbers, but may encounter situations where expected numbers are absent from the dropdown. The UX must help users understand why a number is unavailable (not a call queue member, not assigned to their account) and provide a path to request access from an admin.
key_points: Selects outbound caller ID from a dropdown of available numbers
Can only see numbers from their account, company main number, or their call queues
May not understand why certain call queue numbers are absent from the dropdown
Must contact admin if they need access to a call queue number

task_flows.title: Configure Auto-Receptionist Caller ID to Prevent Call Failures
task_flows.summary: Admins diagnose and fix call failures caused by auto-receptionist caller ID misconfiguration by navigating to the user's profile and selecting the appropriate outbound caller ID number. The flow includes a post-configuration testing step, which is critical for verification. Designers should consider inline validation or proactive warnings when a risky configuration is detected.
key_points: Path: Phone System Management > Users & Rooms > Users > [user] > Profile > Outbound Caller ID
Admin selects the auto-receptionist number (or alternative) from the dropdown
Save is required to apply the change
Manual outbound call testing is the recommended verification step
Troubleshooting references to error codes and anti-spam guidance if calls still fail

task_flows.title: Add Users as Members of a Call Queue
task_flows.summary: Admins add users to a call queue so those users can select the queue's number as their outbound caller ID. The flow goes through Phone System Management > Call Queues > [queue] > Profile > Members, with search and add functionality. The UX challenge is that the relationship between call queue membership and caller ID availability may not be intuitive to admins.
key_points: Path: Phone System Management > Call Queues > [queue] > Profile > Members > View or Edit
Search for existing members or add new users via search box
Users tab available for adding users not currently in the queue
Changes take effect immediately; no additional save step for membership

task_flows.title: Add a Call Queue Number to a Phone User's Profile
task_flows.summary: Admins add a specific call queue phone number to a user's outbound caller ID options via the user's profile. This can include additional custom numbers beyond the standard set. The flow requires navigating to the user rather than the call queue, which means admins may need to switch between different management interfaces to fully configure caller ID.
key_points: Path: Phone System Management > Users & Rooms > Users > [user] > Profile > Outbound Caller ID
Select call queue number from dropdown, then Save
Optional 'Customize Numbers > Add' for additional phone numbers
Both account numbers and call queue numbers can be added as custom options

constraints.title: Outbound Caller ID Dropdown Display Limit
constraints.summary: The outbound caller ID dropdown is capped at 100 phone numbers maximum. For power users or organizations with many call queues, this means some assigned numbers may not be visible in the dropdown. Designers should consider how to handle overflow scenarios with search, filtering, or pagination within the selection UI.
key_points: Maximum of 100 phone numbers shown in the outbound caller ID dropdown
Numbers beyond 100 are effectively invisible to the user
No mention of search or filter within the dropdown to mitigate the limit
Particularly impactful for users in many call queues

constraints.title: E.164 Format Cannot Be Disabled
constraints.summary: E.164 international formatting, including country codes, is always applied to caller ID and cannot be disabled or customized. This means local-format caller ID display is not achievable through Zoom Phone settings alone, and the actual display depends on the recipient's device behavior.
key_points: Country code prefixes are always included in caller ID
No configuration option to display local format only
Japanese +81 prefix specifically noted as immutable
Display outcome depends on recipient's device, carrier, and settings

constraints.title: Call Forwarding Obscures Original Caller ID
constraints.summary: When calls are forwarded to external numbers, the forwarding source number is displayed rather than the original caller's number. This is a significant UX consideration for call routing flows where preserving caller identity is important for recipients.
key_points: Forwarded calls show the forwarding number, not the original caller
Applies to external forwarding destinations
Cross-tenant forwarding between organizations also loses original caller info
No configuration workaround mentioned in the article

constraints.title: Caller ID Selection Restriction by Association
constraints.summary: Users can only select outbound caller IDs from numbers assigned to their own account, the company main number, or call queues where they are members. This restriction means the available set of caller ID options is dynamic based on the user's organizational relationships and can change as memberships are updated.
key_points: Only numbers from: user's account, company main number, or call queues the user belongs to
Call queue numbers require explicit membership, not just assignment
Numbers from call queues the user is not a member of are hidden from the dropdown
Changes in call queue membership dynamically affect available caller ID options

ux_patterns.title: Restricted Dropdown with Invisible Options
ux_patterns.summary: The outbound caller ID dropdown only shows numbers the user is permitted to use, silently hiding unavailable options without explanation. This creates a discoverability problem where users may not realize certain numbers exist or understand why they cannot access them. A more transparent pattern would show unavailable options as disabled with explanatory tooltips.
key_points: Dropdown shows only permitted numbers, not all possible numbers
No indication that additional numbers exist but are unavailable
Users must infer or ask admin why a number is missing
100-item cap adds another layer of potential invisibility

ux_patterns.title: Troubleshooting Follow-Through References
ux_patterns.summary: When a configuration change does not resolve an issue, the article directs users to additional resources (error codes, anti-spam guidance, network/firewall configuration) rather than providing inline troubleshooting. This multi-resource pattern means the UX for problem resolution is fragmented across multiple articles and requires users to self-navigate to the next step.
key_points: End-of-flow links to related troubleshooting articles
No inline diagnostic steps beyond the primary configuration change
References error codes, anti-spam guidance, and network configuration as next steps
Users must self-identify which follow-up resource applies to their situation
